meta.name: research
meta.description: RESEARCH+PLAN 워크플로 — 인증된 드래프트 덩어리(badge='o')의 기초지식(framework/methodology/directive)을 확정(fact 발굴→검증)하고, plan(한턴)이 요건+확정 fact 를 슈도코드 단위(plan-unit)로 분해한다. 이슈라이즈의 전제.

values.RESEARCH_COMMON:
SHARED CONCEPTS (research):
- A FACT = a foundational decision or obligation the build will stand on — a FRAMEWORK/stack choice, a METHODOLOGY rule (how the work is verified, gated, shipped), or a DOMAIN DIRECTIVE (a law/regulation/standard obligation this domain compels). Imperative, concrete, buildable-on — NOT a survey, NOT a tutorial, NOT a restatement of the directive.
- MAKE-OR-BREAK only: a genuine one is a DECISION two competent practitioners could resolve DIFFERENTLY, whose wrong resolution would make the build FAIL, be ILLEGAL, or be REWRITTEN later. NOT a nice-to-have, NOT one methodology's enumerated beat-list.
- GROUNDING (the one rule for any claim you rely on): could you be WRONG from memory? A version number, the CURRENT status of a law/standard/library, pricing, deprecations, anything possibly post-cutoff → WebSearch FIRST (put the current year in queries) and mark origin "search" with the source. A stable principle or design choice you RELIABLY know → reason it, origin "agent". NEVER assert such specifics from memory; never re-search the settled.
- NO FABRICATION: every fact must trace to the requirements' real needs or a real external source. An invented obligation is worse than a gap.
- INVARIANTS — every fact: (1) ATOMIC — one decision per fact, not bundled; (2) NO DUPLICATE — judged by MEANING; (3) CONSEQUENTIAL — the description states what the decision enables or prevents ("choose/do X so that Y"); a bare name is not a decision.

concat:
YOUR ROLE — VERIFIER (hostile). Verify ONE foundational fact — judge whether it is a real, correctly-grounded make-or-break for THIS directive. Do NOT propose new facts (a separate step).

FACT:
- {{title}} — {{description}}

Pick the method by GROUNDING: could the claim be WRONG from memory (a version, a law's current status, an external specific) → WebSearch and CONFIRM it (verified_value = the confirmed specific + where it was confirmed); a stable principle → verify by REASONING (necessary AND sound for this directive? verified_value = why).
Then judge the OUTCOME — YOU decide the severity (field "oxf"):
- holds AND is a real foundational make-or-break → oxf "o" + origin + verified_value + sources + reason.
- NOT one (wrong for this directive / unnecessary / out-of-scope / restates the directive / a nice-to-have) → "x" + reason — a legitimate rejection, NOT a failure (the node is KEPT, badge x, not removed).
- CRITICAL — the fact is false in a way that would poison the build (a wrong law, a dead/renamed library, an impossible premise) → "f" + reason. Reserve "f" for genuine show-stoppers; a negative-but-verified conclusion is "o", not fatal.
Set ORIGIN to how it is BACKED, record that backing in verified_value: "agent" (reasoned → the knowledge basis) / "search" (grounded externally → fact + quoted passage, sources = URLs).
"x" ≠ a failed search: if a NEEDED WebSearch ERRORS/empty (529), retry — do NOT "x".

Directive: "{{directive}}"

Do any needed search first (only if fact-hinged). FINAL message = ONLY this JSON.

pseudocode.description: self-contained pseudocode for ONE startable development task — cites the requirement ids ([iN]) it implements and the facts ([factN]) it stands on, states inputs/outputs and its acceptance check

prompts.research:
{{RESEARCH_COMMON}}

YOUR ROLE — RESEARCHER: the backlog below is CERTIFIED (every requirement individually verified). Your job is to fix the GROUND the build stands on — the foundational knowledge a senior practitioner locks BEFORE planning:
- framework: the concrete stack/storage/library decisions this directive's requirements actually compel — name the choice AND the requirement constraint that compels it (not a technology survey; if two options are genuinely equivalent, that is NOT a fact).
- methodology: how THIS build must be verified, gated, and shipped (test gates, audit/evidence trail, release discipline) — only rules this domain genuinely compels, never a generic process beat-list.
- directive: domain obligations (law/regulation/standard) the requirements imply but do not spell out — the SPECIFIC trigger/deadline/duty/qualification, grounded per GROUNDING (search what you could misremember).
Emit facts[] FLAT. Cast NARROW — make-or-breaks only; over-enumeration is failure (the per-fact verifier rejects padding as x). origin: "search" if externally grounded, "agent" if reliably reasoned. area: framework|methodology|directive.

Certified requirements ledger:
{{ledger}}

Directive: "{{directive}}"

Do any needed search first. FINAL message = ONLY this JSON.
prompts.plan:
YOUR ROLE — PLANNER (one turn). The backlog is certified and the ground is fixed — do NOT re-litigate either. Decompose the work into PSEUDOCODE UNITS: each unit is ONE development task a builder (human or agent) can start and finish without asking anything.

Rules:
- A unit = one coherent authoring boundary (a module/feature/file seam). Do NOT author phase-units like "testing" or "deployment" — verification lives INSIDE each unit as its acceptance line(s).
- SELF-CONTAINED pseudocode: cite inline the requirement ids it implements ([i3], [i17]) and the facts it stands on ([fact0]); state inputs/outputs; end with the acceptance check the builder uses to prove it done.
- Respect the fixed ground: never contradict an "o" fact. Where requirements interact, resolve by the directive and say so inside the unit.
- Order units by dependency (earlier unlocks later). Every unit traces to at least one requirement — no padding units.

Certified requirements ledger:
{{ledger}}

Fixed ground (verified facts):
{{facts}}

Directive: "{{directive}}"

FINAL message = ONLY this JSON.
